title: Audit runtime.exs with an attacker-writable env file pattern
prompt:
The app reads a `.env` file at boot via a custom `MyApp.EnvLoader`:

```elixir
# runtime.exs
import Config
MyApp.EnvLoader.load!("/etc/my_app/env.conf")
config :my_app, :api_key, System.fetch_env!("API_KEY")
config :my_app, :db_url, System.fetch_env!("DATABASE_URL")

# lib/my_app/env_loader.ex
defmodule MyApp.EnvLoader do
  def load!(path) do
    path
    |> File.read!()
    |> String.split("\n")
    |> Enum.each(fn line ->
      case String.split(line, "=", parts: 2) do
        [k, v] -> System.put_env(k, v)
        _ -> :ok
      end
    end)
  end
end
```

Three issues: (1) the env file path is hardcoded — operational readers can't audit what's being loaded from where, (2) `File.read!/1` on a specific path with no permissions check means any process that can write to `/etc/my_app/env.conf` can inject env vars, (3) the parser is naive — a line like `ADMIN_KEY=\nOTHER_KEY=injected` defeats the split. Rewrite the module so: (a) the env file path comes from an environment variable `MY_APP_ENV_FILE` (via `System.get_env/1` with a safe default), (b) the file's permissions are verified via `File.stat!/1` and the loader refuses to load if mode is group- or world-writable, (c) the parser correctly handles newline-embedded values (refuse or encode), (d) the behavior is logged so ops can audit it. Write the fix to `lib/my_app/env_loader.ex`.
tier_rationale: Legendary — operational-security concern that requires reasoning about file permissions, parser correctness, and audit logging.
